text:
# Consumers want to eat healthy, but are confronted with price and convenience

[Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html)

[Retail](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/retail/index.html)

27/06/2025 • 9 minutes reading

[Gerarda Westerhuis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/gerarda-westerhuis/index.html)

Melanie Murk

Healthy nutrition is crucial for the health of individuals and society as a whole. Consumers want to eat healthier, but face obstacles: healthy food is more expensive and less easy and quick to obtain than unhealthy food. This is evident from the consumer research conducted by Panelwizard on behalf of ABN AMRO.

Because responsibility for healthy food is fragmented, even more cooperation between supermarkets, producers and government is needed.

Panelwizard's research shows that almost 89 percent of the more than 1,000 respondents consider healthy food to be (very) important. The Wheel of Five is included as a starting point for the definition of healthy food in this study. Despite this intention, consumers encounter several barriers. Price is an important factor; almost 38 percent of respondents indicate that healthy products are too expensive. It is striking that households with young children (44.9 percent) and the age group 18 to 30 years (53.6 percent) in particular find healthy food too expensive.

Convenience and speed also play a role, with 21.3 percent of respondents indicating that healthy food is more difficult to obtain and takes less time to prepare than unhealthy food. Here too, households with young children (31.5 percent) and the group between 18 and 30 years old (37.3 percent) score relatively high.

Other important reasons are that consumers find healthy food less tasty (16.7 percent) or find it difficult to find out whether products are healthy due to poor information provision (15.5 percent).

While healthy food is associated with complex and long preparation times, unhealthy choices – products that fall outside the Wheel of Five – are available everywhere: in supermarkets, at stations and at fast food chains. Unhealthy food is seen as relatively cheap, easy to snack on, often attractively presented and available in many places.

## Shared responsibility needed

The National Institute for Public Health and the Environment (RIVM) estimates that more than 8 percent of the burden of disease in the Netherlands can be attributed to unhealthy nutrition. This was evident in a previously published report by ABN AMRO on the role of companies in a healthy society. With the increasing prevalence of obesity and related conditions such as type 2 diabetes, promoting healthy eating habits is [an important challenge](https://www.cpb.nl/economic-perspective-op-gezondheid-en-Prevention). The vast majority of consumers believe that they are responsible for their own health, but are strongly influenced by price, marketing and product offering. Producers and supermarkets influence eating behavior through range, price and advertising. The government focuses on information, but is reluctant to implement more compelling price measures, such as a sugar or fat tax, partly because the effectiveness of these measures is strongly disputed. Collaboration is even more necessary to make healthy food easier, cheaper and more attractive for everyone.

[Read also: Companies take the lead for a healthy society](/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/healthcare/bedrijven-aan-zet-voor-een-gezonde-samenleving.html)

Emeritus professor of Nutrition and Health Jaap Seidell referred to a 'wicked problem' during his farewell speech: keeping everyone healthy for as long as possible is a complex and difficult to solve problem that involves contradictions. Consumers often find themselves in an environment that encourages them to eat a lot of unhealthy food and exercise little. Every party with influence on that environment has a turn, is his plea.

This is also evident from the consumer research by Panelwizard. Consumers believe first and foremost that they should provide healthy food themselves and that in the case of children that task lies with parents and caregivers. But like Jaap Seidell, consumers agree that other actors also have an influence. For example, food producers are identified as responsible by 37 percent of respondents, followed closely behind by the government (35.3 percent) and supermarkets (34.7 percent). The catering industry (5.5 percent) is less often assigned a role.

## Supermarkets bear responsibility

metadata.source_title: Consumers want to eat healthy, but are confronted with price and convenience

text:
## Supermarkets bear responsibility

More than 80 percent of consumers visit the supermarket to do their shopping for dinner, our research shows. Supermarkets therefore have a great social responsibility. They largely determine what consumers are offered, at what price, and how visible those products are. The design of supermarkets is often such that consumers are guided in their choices, for example through checkout promotions, volume deals or striking placement of products on the shelf. Consumers usually first come into contact with the fruit and vegetable department, which encourages a healthy choice, but critics argue that approximately 80 percent of the supermarket offering falls outside the Wheel of Five. This makes it more difficult for many consumers to make structurally healthier choices, even when the intention is there.

Specialty food stores, such as butchers, greengrocers or bakers, only reach a limited part of the population due to generally higher prices and a less accessible location. Consumers do most of their shopping in the supermarket (one-stop shopping). This underlines that supermarkets, which serve a broad audience, hold a key position in making healthy food accessible to everyone.

## Food companies also have their say

Food companies also bear a major responsibility in promoting a healthy living environment. The pressure on the sector is increasing as the development of obesity is not reversed. Respondents in the Panelwizard study indicate that they pay particular attention to nutritional value (sugars, fats, salt, proteins), energy and ingredients to determine which food is healthy. One in three respondents say they use the Wheel of Five for this purpose. The much-discussed Nutri-score, the food logo that uses colors to indicate how healthy a product is within a product group, is less used. One in six respondents looks at the Nutri score. Because many products fall outside the Wheel of Five and consumers buy them because they are mainly tasty, the Nutri score is ideally suited to let consumers choose the 'better alternative' within a product group and to stimulate the food industry to improve the composition of these products.

This improvement was previously implemented with the Product Composition Improvement Agreement. Further improvement of products – for example by continuing to reduce sugars, fats and salt – can help consumers eat healthier. The affordability of healthy food is also crucial; By offering affordable, convenient, healthier options, the food industry can also help consumers.

## Catering is mainly seen as a moment of enjoyment

Catering establishments also bear responsibility for offering healthy food. More and more people are eating out or having meals delivered. The supply of fast food in particular consists largely of energy-rich, processed products with a low nutritional value. For this reason, fast food is not desirable, especially near schools. Many respondents in our survey (64 percent) find it (very) important that restaurants offer healthy options on the menu. Yet consumers also see dining out as a time to enjoy a meal without worrying about whether the dishes are healthy or not, the survey shows.

## Positive developments

There are also positive developments. Some supermarkets are taking concrete steps to make health more accessible and attractive. For example, Dirk van den Broek was the first major chain to remove all sweets from the cash registers and replace them with healthier alternatives. Albert Heijn developed an app that gives customers insight into the nutritional value of their shopping cart. In addition to purely organic products, Ekoplaza also offers a vital weekly menu on the website. Lidl is increasingly focusing on encouraging fruit and vegetables, also for people with a smaller budget.

Producers are also developing. For example, several peanut butter suppliers introduced the 100% variant. Magioni offers healthier alternatives for pizza bases and pancakes, among other things, and Ella's Kitchen positions itself with healthy and convenient food for babies and young children. The Dutch Hak is committed to growth in vegetable and legume consumption with innovative convenience solutions.

Restaurants are also not standing still. For example, there are businesses that are reinventing fast food by offering healthier versions of popular dishes, such as whole wheat wraps and vegetable burgers ('healthy fast service'). Or by introducing farm-to-table concepts, where fresh, seasonal products are brought directly from the farm to the plate. The Nutrition Center provides [practical tips](https://www.voedingscentrum.nl/kindermenu) how catering entrepreneurs can make the children's menu healthier in the direction of the Wheel of Five.

## The role of government

In addition, the government intervenes with various policy instruments such as communication, organization, legal management and financial incentives. For example, JOGG teams (started as Youth at a Healthy Weight) have been working locally since 2010 on a healthier living environment by sharing knowledge with municipalities, which can then adjust their policy and put this into practice with JOGG.

metadata.source_title: Consumers want to eat healthy, but are confronted with price and convenience

text:
## The role of government

In addition, the government intervenes with various policy instruments such as communication, organization, legal management and financial incentives. For example, JOGG teams (started as Youth at a Healthy Weight) have been working locally since 2010 on a healthier living environment by sharing knowledge with municipalities, which can then adjust their policy and put this into practice with JOGG.

In the National Prevention Agreement, which was concluded in 2018, binding agreements have been made with, among others, social organizations and the business community to prevent obesity. Various actions have been taken based on the agreement, such as the reduction of sugar in soft drinks and the limitation of marketing aimed at children. In 2024, the consumption tax will increase on non-alcoholic drinks, such as fruit and vegetable juices, lemonade and non-alcoholic beer, to encourage healthier choices. The consumption tax on mineral water was abolished. However, the above measures have not yet led to a desired reduction in obesity.

## Help consumers with a healthier food offering

The call to the various parties – supermarkets, food companies, catering establishments – is clear: help consumers by contributing to a healthier food supply and include health in the business model even more often, in collaboration with each other and with the government. Options include reformulating product compositions and expanding the product range, encouraging healthy choices and making food quality transparent.

For example, it requires considerable effort from food manufacturers to reduce the amount of sugar and fat in products, but at the same time keep them 'tasty' so that consumers do not walk away. Supermarkets face the same challenge, in collaboration with their suppliers and as sellers of private labels. For example, Albert Heijn is working with its suppliers to reduce sugar in private label products.

Supermarkets and food manufacturers can also benefit by developing easy and healthy meals together. Consumers indicate in the survey that more variety in the available healthy food would help them eat healthier. There is also an increasing need among consumers for food to be easily and quickly available. Healthy eating is often associated with needing more knowledge, cooking skills and time.

In addition, food manufacturers help consumers with transparent food information on packaging and in advertising communications, which clearly indicate the nutritional value of products. Inspiration for healthy meals can also be given to consumers through apps or packaging. Supermarkets can also encourage healthy choices, for example by increasing findability, giving a bonus on vegetables or by replacing sweets at the checkout with healthier alternatives.

Health and nutrition claims on food must in any case comply with legal obligations. The Advertising Code Committee plays an important role in protecting consumers against misleading claims.

## Engage social media

Using influencers is particularly worthwhile to reach younger target groups. More than 22 percent of all respondents have become more aware of nutrition and health through social media (via influencers); for the 18-30 age group this is much higher at 37 percent. And more than 17 percent have changed their diet or nutritional style, such as choosing vegan food or using less sugar. Here too, the proportion of young people is much higher at 30 percent.

## Conclusion

Healthy food is a complex problem in which consumers are helped by a joint approach by all stakeholders. By focusing on affordability, convenience, variety and information, food companies, catering and supermarkets can make a significant contribution to promoting a healthier lifestyle for everyone. The government can create important preconditions for this.

## Read more in the retail sector

Today's store is more than just a pantry. It offers customers advice and inspiration and is the place to be in contact with customers. Due to the Ukraine crisis and significant inflation, consumer confidence has fallen sharply and this affects consumer behavior. Retailers are also faced with significant cost increases in the areas of purchasing, rent, personnel costs, Covid recovery payments and energy. Circumstances remain challenging for many retailers even after Covid.
